speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape seven with Irvin Eisner. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I read in your book, and you were telling me when we met that whilst you've been involved in education, you were elected to go on the March of the Living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we would like to hear from you how this affected you and how the children responded.

speaker: interviewee
text: To me, March of the Living-- whoever organized it, I have to tell him [NON-ENGLISH], a thank you, because it is very, very, very important for youngsters not only to read, but to see. Even though you can see exactly what has happened, but there's plenty more to see, plenty of evidence. I, myself, being a survivor-- I never knew til I went back as a [NON-ENGLISH] the disaster was so terribly-- even though that old Poland is a [NON-ENGLISH], a cemetery, but you can feel the outcry. I told my students, 'You will never probably see what I'm going to see. You are never going to hear from the graves what I am going to hear. But you will see enough.' Even though I wasn't there for 50 years, but I imagine. And with and we saw more that I did imagine. And we had sessions, six of them, before we went to those camps. And we prepared those youngsters-- in different-- different sessions, different topics. And if I will-- the time doesn't allow. I have letters here from girls from the States and from here where they wrote me feedback from the camps. The general line is 'Mr. Eisner, we became better Jews, stronger human beings. In those two weeks, we learned more than all our years about Jewish history than was in major writings about the Holo-- about the March of the Living.' For me, it was a conclusion, from my first book, til what I saw 50 years later. And I told them, what tremendous mitzvah we are going to perform. And mind you, most as those students were not Orthodox. Once-- or maybe a quarter only were Orthodox people.

speaker: interviewer
text: They listened, and I explained to them, you know, when those people went to their graves, which were [INAUDIBLE], there was no one to accompany them. God forbid, if somebody dies today-- a father, a mother, brother, sister-- there is a [NON-ENGLISH]. There is a funeral. Family goes. Til the last drop of Earth, we are beside them. We go home. We say kaddish. Do you know that after those 6 million people, there's hardly one who can say kaddish? We are the ones who came here to say kaddish. Isn't that the mitzvah? One of the mitzvot which the Talmud says is that you are rewarded in this world and in the other world. [NON-ENGLISH]. The company of death. Who accompanied those people? We came now to fulfill the mitzvot of [NON-ENGLISH]. We came to say a Kaddish and to tell the world, [NON-ENGLISH]. We are here in Poland. The ground may be soaked with blood, but we are here standing on the graves of the 6 million telling them we are here. We are your descendants. We are your [NON-ENGLISH], your grandchildren. We came to tell you that your death was not in vain. You cried to me. I was with you when in Warsaw, but I am standing right now saying a Kaddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: You told me to each of us, if you survived, please don't forget us. I remember your cry, and I am here to tell you I didn't forget you. Together with 140 students and 6,000 together with us. We came to say for you, [NON-ENGLISH]. We don't forget you. Because of your blood it helped the new growing up. We have a new state, we have [NON-ENGLISH] Israel. Your blood helped for us to give us a country. And the reason we have schools, and thousands of children are going to schools-- it is because of you. Your death was not in vain.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from here, from Poland, I am telling the world, never again. You came to do [NON-ENGLISH]. A true kindness. What is a true kindness? A true kindness means you do a kindness to a person, you never can get it back. It's the only way you can do [NON-ENGLISH] is to dead people, because you can't wait for them to help you back, to give you back what you are doing to them. And we are performing tremendous [NON-ENGLISH] mitzvot. [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we are taking with us, we are going to take with us, all the 6 million. I am taking my mother, my three sisters, and my brother. I am going to bury them in [? Erev ?] [? Yisrael ?] in our land-- not in Poland. In our land beside my father, [NON-ENGLISH]. When I went to Birkenau, I took some sand from there for my mother, and brothers, and so on. I went to Majdanek from the Hill of Ashes. I took some ashes for the 6 million. And I took them in my pocket and I buried them in [? Erev ?] [? Yisrael. ?] This is why we came. To tell the [NON-ENGLISH], the martyrs, we are here. So a new generation grew up. It's your children, your grandchildren, your brothers and sisters. We are one.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you something. Music. We were talking earlier about the fact that you are a very happy man, and music is one of the arts that does bring joy. When you went on the March of the Living, you meet someone, a musician who had played in Auschwitz. Would you tell us about that? I'll tell you-- I'll tell you two different musics. We speak about music. There's one music where my principal, Mr. Spitzer, played a violin in Birkenau while people were marching to the death. It was one kind of music.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a music in Auschwitz where six musicians were playing a whole day. [NON-ENGLISH] while people were marching to death. While those survivors were playing the same music, while we were now marching the same stage from Auschwitz to Birkenau, again the same music. But now it's played music for living people. People are going to live. People are going to build. People are going to make flourish. People are going to continue as Jews, while the other music was music of death. And to me rings that music of happiness. I don't want to listen to the music of death. I wanted the music of happiness. And I put up a tape on my tape recorder. I am dancing. I am dancing because that makes me happy. And I will never forget that scene. Playing music for people marching to death, and playing music people marching to life. That makes me happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were on this experience on the March of the Living, you were able to remember the songs from your childhood? And you sang a song to your children that you love and you teach. And I wondered if you would sing a song for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll try. What happened was, you see, every-- after we traveled-- I mean, we were very, very tiring. From 5:00 in the morning until 10:00 in the evening. And then still we had a session every night to sum up what we have seen. And we gave all these for students to say their experience from their grandparents, and so on, and so on. And in one place, I remember a girl who had a note her parents left in Kielce. And in Kielce received a pogrom after the war if you know what happened. And I said to her, from our school I said, in honor of your great-grandparents who were killed in Kielce, I will sing you a song. Not called-- we have a song called, [? Bells. ?] 'My Shtetl of [? Bells.' ?] And I said, I will sing you the same song, but instead of [? Bells, ?] it's going to be Kielce. And I sang, and they sang with me. [SINGING] My shtetl of [? Bells. ?] [NON-ENGLISH] [? bells. ?] My shtetl of Kielce. [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: And sung many more. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I sang and I spoke Yiddish, because that's the language they lived. That's the language they died. That's how they spoke. That's how they died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Apart from the wonderful teaching that you've done over the years, you have written books. And I believe you have written a poem in honor of your mother, and that you would like to read that for us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with a bitter heart. But happy again, I will read it for you that poem, written by Irving Eisner, a survivor of the Holocaust during the March of the Living. April 19, '94 on the way from Krakow to Birkenau in Poland. My mother. I am back after 50 years to look for my sisters and brother. To look for the 6 million [NON-ENGLISH]-- among them my dear mother. Here in Birkenau they were torn away from me forever. Find them I will never. Here in the gas chambers and crematoriums they all have perished, have gone. I search. I look on the grounds of Birkenau, Auschwitz. But I find none. My heart is broken, filled with devastation and pain. I know that my mission is unfortunately in vain. I went to Majdanek, to Treblinka to look at least for some tombstones, a Kaddish, a prayer to say. But there is no sign, no grave of our [NON-ENGLISH]. There is no one whom a prayer to say. A pile of [? fascists ?] in Majdanek. Gas chambers, crematoriums, and self-made ditches are left. To tell the devastations of death of our martyrs, [NON-ENGLISH]. They are gone forever, [NON-ENGLISH]. I came to take you, dear Mama, to take my three sisters and brother. The 6 million [NON-ENGLISH]. To take your souls and bones to our beautiful land of Israel where you will live forever and ever. I promise not to forget you. Never, never. [NON-ENGLISH]. My eyes are swollen from tears, my heart in pain. But I believe that your death was not in vain. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mr. Eisner. Is there any other poem that you would like to share with us otherwise?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I have-- I have written a lot. And really all my stories. In order not to forget, I also published two books because I want people-- I didn't know that there was going to be videos. And I'm very thankful for doing this, but I want also people-- all of my stories I'm telling are written even more what I could tell. I just want to read you one when I came back after 50 years. I didn't cry in the ghetto of Warsaw in the year 1944. But I did cry a lot in the year 1994. I didn't cry at the gas chambers of Birkenau 50 years ago, but I was yelling, shouted, and cried bitterly 50 years later. I didn't see or hear my mother standing beside me and crying for 50 years. But I did see her, and I did hear her 50 years later. I did not choke from the gases and from the smoke in Birkenau in 1994. But I felt them very much in 1994. I didn't know too much about the slaughter, [? house ?] of Treblinka 50 years ago. But I saw the many thousands of tombstones, the enormous [NON-ENGLISH] graveyard 50 years later. I didn't enter the gas chambers and the crematorium Majdanek in 1944. But I did light candles there in the ovens in 1994. I didn't share or see the joy of my people before the war in Warsaw, Krakow, Lublin, Kielce, [NON-ENGLISH] and so on. But I did say Kaddish on their graves. I didn't realize the catastrophe, the tragedy, and the devastation of our people in Europe was so terrible, so intense in 1944. But I see it and unfortunately realize it in the year 1994. I didn't understand or comprehend the shoah 50 years ago. I don't understand it today. And I am more confused 50 years later. No, I didn't cry in 1994. I cried bitterly in 1994. Bewildered, confused, and frightened. March of the Living, by survivor Irving Eisner, April 1994. That is a truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Eisner, I must thank you for your most wonderful and moving testimony today. And before we conclude in a short while, I wanted to ask you if there's anything that I haven't asked you that you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to say to my people, they're all my children. Students is like children. And all of the Jewish people. [NON-ENGLISH]. Be proud. Even though it's hard but be proud. Don't give up. Be strong. Be strong. The stronger we are, the more respected we will be. Strong. Strong. [NON-ENGLISH]. Never, never give up. And don't dare to forget. [NON-ENGLISH]. Never, never again. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mr. Eisner. You're welcome.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I remember, this is my grandfather, Nathan Nosen Katz. And this is a picture from the first World War. He was a soldier. Austria-Hungary solider. This is my father, [NON-ENGLISH], when he jumped from the train a second time in Budapest. And he ran around, and he tried to make for himself gentile papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph Eisner. That's my father. That's me, the taller one, Irving Eisner. And from our high school, in Hebrew high school in [NON-ENGLISH]. The little sister is [? Yitelah, ?] who perished in Birkenau when she was 10 years old. And that's my brother, Nachum, who was 12. He also perished in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: The older man, obviously it's my father, [NON-ENGLISH], Joseph Eisner from Soimy or [? Soima. ?] And beside him that's myself, living Eisner. We are sitting in Pocking or [NON-ENGLISH] DP camp, and thinking to ourselves, we lost the families, we lost a lot of our family, what's next? What are we going to do next?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my beautiful bride to become my wife. The wedding in Pocking, in Germany. It is ironic that the wedding took place just not far from one of the camps where I was tortured in [NON-ENGLISH], right now from [NON-ENGLISH]. From sorrow to joy. My bride's name is [? Seymo, ?] or Emma Eisner. Before her maiden name was [? Seymo, ?] or Emma Goodman. This is my new family. Nathan, who was born in Israel. On the left side, the little one, left side is Moishechaim. Moishe was born also in Israel. And the little one in the middle is my baby son, Murray, who was born in-- in Toronto. I think Nathan is about 11, Moishe is about 8, and the little one is about 3. This is one of the many, many pictures of my school where I was teaching. This is 1953. Those are my beloved students, or my beloved children, from 1961 here in Toronto. This is a picture of 1993, also in our school. That is the last class I was teaching before becoming a supervisor or a principal. In fact, I can see the bottom line, the third-- the girl-- is my own granddaughter, [? Haia. ?] [? Haia ?] Eisner. This is my lovely family who moved to live in our land Israel. In the center you have Nathan with the glasses. My son Nathan, my oldest son. Beside him is Josie, Josepha. [? Haia ?] Eisner, [? Euri ?] Eisner, and [? Amnad ?] Eisner. And Josie was called, her maiden name was Teller.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that from the left or the right, Mr. Eisner, that you were telling us their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started with the middle. The parents were Nathan and Josepha. The oldest son, the oldest was [? Euri. ?] Then comes [? Haia ?] and [? Amnad. ?] This is the second gate in Birkenau. The trains arrived to the first gate for selection. And there was another selection taken place here. And from here the people went to the gas chambers. This picture was taken in the March of the Living, April 1994. This is in one of the barracks, original barracks-- though they all look the same-- in Treblinka. And here I almost collapsed. And I stood up. I lit the candles in memory of my mother, and sisters and brother who perished here in Birkenau-- the Yahrzeit candles-- in 1994, Much of the Living in April. This is the-- the people are sitting there. You can't see them on top of [? Mila, ?] [? 18 ?] in Warsaw. There's a bunker where was the last freedom fighters were hiding. And Mordechai Anielewicz, their leader, was killed here. I'm singing with them, [NON-ENGLISH]. The partisan song. Warsaw, 1994. March of the Living. This is a camp known as the death camp of Treblinka-- the monument. And you can in the back is the beautiful forest. And in it is the terrible 480,000 Jews buried here. I am blowing the shofar. March of the Living, April 1994. This is the Nozyk Synagogue, the only Synagogue left in Warsaw. And as a survivor of Warsaw, I was honored to lead the people in [NON-ENGLISH]. March of the Living, April 1994. In order not to forget the tragedy of our people, the shoah, I was asked by students and by people to record my memories. So about 10 years ago, I wrote where I register all the daily events as much as I remember them. In other words, a fire of the camps. My second book, I wrote just in 1995. Recording my experience going back to the camps. I am-- I am very happy to have my family around me, and we hope to continue so for many, many years. I am just kind of sorry that my other son Nathan and family cannot be here. But we also-- we took pictures from them as well. I want to introduce my family. From my right is Moishe, Moishechaim Eisner, his lovely wife, Kim, his oldest son, Adam, his beautiful daughter, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Nadia. I'm sorry for-- it's kind of harder [? with ?] many kids. And the little one, called Justin. And actually, my beloved wife, [? you've ?] already made pictures from our wedding. Next is my youngest son, Murray Eisner, his lovely wife Cindy, and their beautiful little son, Mitchel. And the little girl, Jessica. And beside me is my oldest grandson, [? Euri. ?] The family is in Israel, his father and mother. [? Euri ?] is the oldest, and Jessica is the youngest. That's my lovely family. KIM (DAUGHTER IN LAW): I'd just like to say that that [INAUDIBLE] Dad has been through in his life has made him a stronger person. And I really believe that it's made him a better parent to my husband, Max. And that I regard both Mom and Dad as my parents. And they're both wonderful people. Thank you.
